name: The Unbelievable Life Of Nisargadatta Maharaj [ynjFKMNwZgw]

shots.narration: Transcription by CastingWords Nisargadatta Maharaj was a simple cigarette seller with a shop in the back alleys of Mumbai, then called Bombay.
Nisargadatta Maharaj was a simple cigarette seller with a shop in the back alleys of Mumbai, then called Bombay. Nisargadatta lived above the shop in a small flat with his wife and four children.

Nisargadatta lived above the shop in a small flat with his wife and four children. A simple circumstance, a simple, humble man. And yet, it was here that seekers would eventually come from all over the planet to hear Nisargadatta speak deeply and profoundly.

And yet, it was here that seekers would eventually come from all over the planet to hear Nisargadatta speak deeply and profoundly. On the nature of self and of the nature of reality itself. Nisargadatta was born Maruti Shivarampant Kamli in 1897 in the Maharashtra district of India. However, when his father died in 1915, Nisargadatta moved the family to Bombay, where with his brother he opened a string of eight small goods shops, selling mainly biris or leaf-rolled cigarettes.
However, when his father died in 1915, Nisargadatta moved the family to Bombay, where with his brother he opened a string of eight small goods shops, selling mainly biris or leaf-rolled cigarettes.

However, when his father died in 1915, Nisargadatta moved the family to Bombay, where with his brother he opened a string of eight small goods shops, selling mainly biris or leaf-rolled cigarettes. In 1923, he married and in the years that followed, he and his wife had three daughters and a son.
In 1923, he married and in the years that followed, he and his wife had three daughters and a son. In 1933, Nisargadatta's life would change forever.
In 1933, Nisargadatta's life would change forever. It was in that year that Nisargadatta met his guru, Siddhar Rameshwar Maharaj.

It was in that year that Nisargadatta met his guru, Siddhar Rameshwar Maharaj. Siddhar Rameshwar Maharaj was a guru in the lineage of the Inchigiri Sampradaya, also known as Nimbargi Sampradaya. This lineage of teachers came from Maharashtra and were... inspired by a number of great spiritual teachers, including the great mystic poet Kabir.
inspired by a number of great spiritual teachers, including the great mystic poet Kabir. The Incaigiri Sampradaya was unique in that it offered spiritual practice to people from
The Incaigiri Sampradaya was unique in that it offered spiritual practice to people from all walks of life, including women and people of all religions. Those who came for the teachings

all walks of life, including women and people of all religions. Those who came for the teachings did not even have to be practicing Hindus. It was particularly popular with householders, those with the responsibilities of work and family. In fact, the gurus of this lineage were known for traveling from home to home to make themselves available to householders,
were known for traveling from home to home to make themselves available to householders, rather than the householder having to make time to visit the guru.
rather than the householder having to make time to visit the guru. Many years later, Nisargadatta would say to his lineage, the Incaigiri Sampradaya,

Many years later, Nisargadatta would say to his lineage, the Incaigiri Sampradaya, I sit here every day answering your questions, but this is not the way that the teachers of my lineage used to do their work. Ours is a householder lineage, which means everyone had to go out and earn his living. There were no meetings like this, where disciples met in large numbers with the

earn his living. There were no meetings like this, where disciples met in large numbers with the guru and asked him questions. Travel was difficult. There were no buses, trains or planes. In the older days, the guru did the traveling on foot, while the disciples stayed at home and looked after their families. The guru walked from village to village to meet the disciples. When the guru knew that he was

families. The guru walked from village to village to meet the disciples. When the guru knew that he was about to pass away, he would appoint one of the householder devotees to be the new guru and that new guru would then take up on the teaching duties, walking from village to village, initiating new devotees and supervising the progress of the old ones. It was this open attitude to all that was evident in the small meeting room in which Nisargadatta met with seekers of all kinds from all over the world. The Guru lineage of the Inchigiri Sampradai would become known throughout the Western world

The Guru lineage of the Inchigiri Sampradai would become known throughout the Western world as Nisargadatta Maharaj became more and more well known around the globe. When Nisargadatta took Siddharameshwar Maharaj as his Guru, Siddharameshwar gave him a mantra and some very straightforward meditation instructions which Nisargadatta followed exactly. In the words of Nisargadatta, My Guru ordered me to attend to the sense I am and to give attention to nothing else. I just obeyed. I did not follow any particular course of breathing, meditation or study of scriptures.

I just obeyed. I did not follow any particular course of breathing, meditation or study of scriptures. Whatever happened, I would turn away my attention from it and remain with the sense I am. It may look too simple, even crude. My only reason for doing it was that my Guru told me so. Yet it worked.
Nisargadatta practiced his Guru's meditation instructions in any spare moment he had between his responsibilities
Nisargadatta practiced his Guru's meditation instructions in any spare moment he had between his responsibilities to his family and to his business.

to his family and to his business. My Guru told me, Go back to that state of pure being where the I am is still in its purity before it got contaminated with this I am or that I am. Your burden is of false identifications. Abandon them all.

Your burden is of false identifications. Abandon them all. I simply followed his instructions which was to focus the mind on pure being, I am, and stay in it. I used to sit for hours together with nothing but the I am in my mind. And soon the peace and joy and deep all-embracing love became my normal state.

And soon the peace and joy and deep all-embracing love became my normal state. In it all disappeared, myself, my Guru, the life I lived and the world around me. Only peace remained and unfathomable silence.

Nisargadatta remained with Siddha Rameshwar Maharaj for merely two and a half years until Siddha Rameshwar's death in 1936 after his Guru's death. Amen. Thank you. Nisargadatta entered a period of intense inquiry and meditation practice.
Nisargadatta entered a period of intense inquiry and meditation practice. He left Mumbai and wandered across India.
After eight months of wandering, Nisargadatta spontaneously decided to return to Mumbai and to his family.
After eight months of wandering, Nisargadatta spontaneously decided to return to Mumbai and to his family.
After eight months of wandering, Nisargadatta spontaneously decided to return to Mumbai and to his family.
It was on this journey home that Nisargadatta realized the self.

It was on this journey home that Nisargadatta realized the self. I realized suddenly and irrevocably that there was nothing wrong anymore. He would spend the rest of his life in Mumbai, keeping only one of his shops, so as to earn an income for his family. In his humble flat, Nisargadatta had a mazzanine was constructed and it was from there he met daily with visitors that came to him. Nisargadatta's lineage of Inchigiri Sampradai had traditionally taught primarily mantra meditation. However, although Nisargadatta often gave a mantra to his students, in his teachings and discourses, Nisargadatta shifted the emphasis from mantra meditation to self-inquiry. Nisargadatta taught what has been called Nisarg Yoga. The primary self-inquiry that Nisargadatta suggested was the one given to him by his Guru and that was to bring attention back consistently to the inherent feeling of existing. In other words, to meditate on the feeling I am. However, Nisargadatta said that I am was merely a gateway. The aim of meditation of the felt sense of I am was to reach the ultimate source prior to the arising of the I am. Nisargadatta called this ultimate source Par Brahma. He said that the feeling of I am arose from conception with the appearance of the body and was maintained by breath and food. Take away breath and or food and the feeling I am would soon disappear. The ultimate reality was not the I am. In fact, I am is not the I am. Maurice Friedman died in 1977 in India with Sri Nisargadatta by his bedside. Of the Westerners who came to Nisargadatta during his teaching years, there are a number who have realized and, even today, continue to communicate Nisargadatta's message. Here are just a few of them. Sailor Bob Adamson. A student of Nisargadatta in the 1970s, Bob Adamson, now in his 90s, continues to this day to receive and teach students in a small flat in Melbourne, Australia. Stephen Howard Wolensky. westerners ramesh was the president of the bank of india when he first met nisarg dutta maharaj after nisarg dutta's death ramesh received students and seekers daily to his mumbai apartment till his

after nisarg dutta's death ramesh received students and seekers daily to his mumbai apartment till his own death in 2009 he authored over 20 books perhaps the two teachers who have had the most effect on western students of Advaitya Vedanta or non-dualism over the last 30 years have been Nisargadatta Maharaj and Sri Ramana Maharishi. Are their teachings in any way similar? Do they complement each other? For many, the answer is yes. Nisargadatta's primary message was,

For many, the answer is yes. Nisargadatta's primary message was, Mediate on the sense of I am until the I am is seen to be illusory. Ramana's primary message was to inquire, Who am I? The inquiry was suggested by Ramana so that one might see very clearly that there was in fact no I present. The one we presume to be thinking our thoughts is actually only itself a thought. Nisargadatta casts that sense of I was a root feeling of I am which is seen as illusory.

a thought. Nisargadatta casts that sense of I was a root feeling of I am which is seen as illusory. We may get a better understanding of what is being pointed to by both teachers if we listen to Ramana's words about his own state. Ramana said that he was in the condition of Jagrath-sushupti. Jagrath is the fully awake state. Susupti is the state of deep dreamless sleep. Ramana said that he was both in Jagrath and Susupti simultaneously. In other words, Jagrath-sushupti. Ramana often pointed to the state of deep dreamless sleep as revealing or pointing to our real being. In fact, Ramana said nothing outside deep dreamless sleep is real and deep dreamless sleep reveals to us the state of true being of our true happiness. Feel or imagine for a moment what Ramana might mean by these words. Feel or imagine the utter bliss of deep dreamless sleep, pure boundless freedom from everything. Is there a worldly bliss that can match it? Now imagine that state of blissful deep dreamless sleep and being profoundly awake and completely and utterly present. Hold on to that. feeling or imagination for a moment. Imagine, out of that state of profound awakeness and the bliss of deep dreamless sleep, that the feeling sense of I am suddenly arises. Can you see or feel why Nisargadatta call I am the primary illusion? Can you see or feel that I am is the root of all sense of separation and the pinching off of our natural and inherent boundless bliss? Can you see that your life can unfold perfectly and harmoniously without the slightest feeling sense of I am? Can you see why Nisargadatta suggested to mediate on the sense of I am until the sense of I am is seen to be illusory? Nisargadatta called the sense of I am Brahma. Nisargadatta called that which is prior to I am Par Brahma. Ramana's state of Jagra Sushupti is the state prior to the arising of the illusory field sense of I am. After three years of intensive meditation on the feeling sense of I am, according to the instructions of his guru, Nisargadatta transcended the feeling sense of I am and awakened to the ultimate reality. Nisargadatta once expressed his realized state in these brief words, there is simply nothing wrong anymore. There is simply nothing wrong anymore.
There is simply nothing wrong anymore.
